ENGNew: Their Crimes Won't End with Just Four?
changeReason: Improved translation from Japanese. Old: "Their Punishment Isn't Over?"

TextENGNew: "No, no, don't worry about it. When you eat oden at this place...@k@vS20/11/440700141.somehow it always ends up on the annual invoice for our gas station contract.@k@vS20/11/440700142.So don't be shy -- have another drink."
The man, red-faced, waved his hand to say he'd had enough and vanished into the brilliant neon lights in front of the station.
"...You should take notes, Kuma-chan. Humble people are the ones who live the longest in the end. Nfu-fu-fu-fu!"
"That story just now... was it really true?"
"Well, who knows. Regardless of whether it's true, I think there's significance in the fact that such a plausible rumor is making the rounds."
changeReason: Old says 'meaning in hearing something that sounds so plausible' (individual experience); new says 'significance in the fact that such a plausible rumor is making the rounds,' matching JPN '流れてる' (circulating) and emphasizing the social phenomenon of the rumor spreading.

TextENGNew: "...Could something like that really be a motive to go after the village chief...?"
changeReason: Old uses 'mayor' while new correctly uses 'village chief' for JPN '村長,' which better reflects Hinamizawa's rural village governance structure.

TextENGNew: "Kuma-chan, I'm always telling you -- a motive only needs to be sufficient for the person in question.@k@vS20/11/440700146.You have to consider differences in values.@k@vS20/11/440700147....Bartender, another round, please."
Despite our efforts to suppress the information, the deaths of Tomitake and Takano were already common knowledge throughout Hinamizawa.
The prevailing rumor was that they had incurred the wrath of Oyashiro-sama for trespassing in the forbidden ritual storehouse.
According to these rumors, two other people had entered alongside them.@k...Shion Sonozaki...@kand Keiichi Maebara.
Voices whispered in the shadows that Oyashiro-sama's curse might befall these two as well.
changeReason: Old says 'those two would be cursed' (stated as certainty); new says 'curse might befall these two' (possibility), correctly reflecting JPN 'あるかもしれない' (might be).

TextENGNew: However, it seemed the blame did not end with the four who had trespassed in the storehouse.
Until last year, it had been secured with formidable locks, but this year it had been changed to a simple one -- wasn't that what allowed intruders to enter so easily?
...That was the rumor going around.
"Have you ever seen it yourself, Kuma-chan? I remember it well from when I went there for something quite a while back."
"Sorry, I can't really recall."
"It was locked up so tight it was almost frightening. Massive iron bars held it firmly shut.@k@vS20/11/440700150.It was sealed up like a bank vault."
But this year, it had been reduced to a single cheap, flimsy padlock.
......The lone girl tasked with guarding the shrine, Rika Furude, had disliked the heavy locks and consulted the village chief...@kwho then had them replaced with a simple padlock.
changeReason: Old uses 'mayor' while new correctly uses 'village chief' for JPN '村長,' consistent with Hinamizawa's rural setting.

TextENGNew: "...So the village chief and Rika Furude share the guilt...? Then... after the chief, wouldn't Rika Furude be in danger next?!"
changeReason: Old uses 'mayor' and 'share the crime' while new uses 'village chief' and 'share the guilt'; JPN '村長' = village chief and '同罪' = equally guilty, making both changes more accurate.

TextENGNew: "That's a strong possibility. ...Kuma-chan.@k@vS20/11/440700152.Contact the unmarked cars patrolling Hinamizawa immediately.@k@vS20/11/440700153.Have them station themselves near Furude Shrine."
changeReason: Old says 'decent possibility' and 'all the cars we have'; new says 'strong possibility' (matching JPN '可能性は大') and 'unmarked cars patrolling' (matching JPN '覆面車' = unmarked cars), both more accurate.

TextENGNew: "R-Roger!!!"
